actions.thought: 7-4 offsuit is one of the weakest possible hands in Hold'em. With no suitedness or connectivity, it has very poor equity even in a three-handed game. Folding is the standard disciplined play to preserve stack depth for better opportunities.

T8 offsuit is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position post-flop. With the Button folding, I am in the Small Blind facing the Big Blind. While completing or attempting a steal are options, T8o lacks the equity and playability to consistently turn a profit when out of position, making folding the most fundamentally sound decision early in the tournament.

I am on the Button (first to act 3-handed pre-flop) with King-Jack offsuit. This is a solid starting hand for short-handed play. I'll open with a standard raise to 2.5x the big blind to try and steal the blinds or play a heads-up pot in position if called.

I have a pocket pair of fives in the small blind. In a three-handed game, a pocket pair is a relatively strong starting hand. The button (Pro) has opened with a standard raise to 250. With stack sizes at 100 big blinds, I have the implied odds to set-mine. While 3-betting is a viable aggressive option to take the initiative, calling allows me to see a flop relatively cheaply and keep the pot controlled while out of position. I will call the 200 more to match the raise.

With 7-6 offsuit in the big blind, I am getting 4:1 pot odds to call. Since I am closing the action and the price is cheap relative to the pot, calling is the most profitable play to see a flop and realize my equity.

I missed my set on an Ace-high board with a Jack as well. This board is much better for my opponents' ranges, particularly the pre-flop raiser (Pro). Being out of position with a small underpair, my best course of action is to check and see how the other players react. I am prepared to fold to a bet as my hand has very little equity against their likely calling or raising ranges.

I have 7-high on a board of As-Jc-2s. There is no reason to bet as I have no equity, and with two opponents, this is a board that likely connects well with the pre-flop raiser's range. Checking is the only viable option to preserve my stack.

With middle pair on an Ace-high board in a multiway pot, checking behind is the best approach. It allows for pot control, avoids check-raises from stronger hands like an Ace or a two-pair, and keeps the bluffing range of my opponents wide for the turn.

The turn King of clubs is another overcard to my pocket fives, making the board Ace-King-Jack-Deuce. My hand has very little showdown value and almost no improvement potential against two opponents on such a coordinated board. Since the flop went check-check-check, there's a small chance someone is trapping or, more likely, everyone is being cautious. Regardless, I cannot profitably bet into this board. I will check and plan to fold if any significant bet is made, as my fives are likely crushed by almost any part of my opponents' ranges.

I hold 7-high on a board of As-Jc-2s-Kc. I have no equity and cannot win against any pair or better, which my opponents likely hold. Betting into two opponents on this board is extremely high risk with no reward, as it will only be called by better hands. Checking is the only logical play, as it allows me to see the river for free without committing more chips to a lost cause.

I improved to two pair (Kings and Jacks) on the turn. Both opponents have checked to me again, suggesting they likely do not have a strong Ace, a better two pair, or a completed straight. I should bet to extract value from one-pair hands like weaker Aces, Kings, or Jacks, as well as various flush and straight draws. A bet of roughly half the pot will charge draws and get calls from worse made hands.

On a board of Ace-Jack-Deuce-King, my pocket fives are extremely vulnerable. The pre-flop raiser (Pro) has bet 400 into a 750 pot after the flop was checked around. This turn card (King) likely improves many hands in a button's opening range (like KQ, KJ, or even slow-played Aces). With another opponent still to act behind me and no real potential for my hand to improve or win at showdown, folding is the most disciplined and mathematically sound play.

I hold 7-6 offsuit with a board of As-Jc-2s-Kc. I have no pair, no draw, and zero equity against the range of the pre-flop raiser who is now betting on the turn. Investing 400 chips into this pot when I cannot win at showdown is a significant mistake. Folding is the only rational decision.

I am on the button with Ten-Deuce of hearts. While suited, this hand is extremely weak and does not warrant an open or a call, especially against a player labeled 'Pro' in the big blind. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack early in the tournament.

I am in the small blind with Jack-Ten suited after the button folded. This is a strong enough hand to raise and take the initiative against the big blind. Raising to 300 exerts pressure and allows me to potentially win the pot pre-flop or take control of the hand with position after the flop.

Facing a 3x raise from the Small Blind, I hold pocket deuces in the Big Blind. We are playing deep-stacked (about 100 BBs effective), which gives me excellent implied odds to set-mine. I also have position post-flop. If I hit a set on the flop, I have the potential to win a massive pot; if I miss, I can easily fold to post-flop aggression. A 3-bet bluff is unnecessary here, so a flat call is the standard and most profitable play.

The board texture is K-K-3 rainbow, which is very dry and favors my pre-flop opening range. However, I have completely missed this flop with Jack-high and have no significant equity. Betting into the Big Blind here is likely to get called by any King or even small pairs like a 3, and I have no real path to victory other than bluffing, which is high-variance on this texture. Checking allows me to control the pot size and re-evaluate on the turn.

With pocket twos on a K-3-K flop, I missed my set and hold very marginal showdown value. The board is incredibly dry. While a bet might fold out some unpaired high cards, it will almost exclusively get called by better hands like higher pocket pairs or a King. Checking behind controls the pot size and allows me to realize my equity by seeing a free turn card.

The board remains very dry (Kc-3h-Ks-7h). My Jack-high has minimal showdown value, and betting here as a pure bluff is unlikely to force a fold from the opponent's range, which likely contains plenty of pairs or an Ace. Checking allows me to control the pot size and keeps me from committing more chips to a hand that is currently behind. I will evaluate the river depending on the opponent's action.

The opponent checks for a second time on this K-K-3-7 board. While betting could deny equity to overcards like Ace-high or Queen-high, pocket twos is essentially the bottom of our made-hand range and has very little value if called. Betting risks getting called by better marginal hands (like 44-99 or Ace-high floating) and reopens the action to a potential check-raise. Checking behind continues to control the pot size and allows us to either get to a cheap showdown or evaluate the river.

I have Jack-high, which has no showdown value and loses to any Ace, King, or even most small pairs that my opponent (Pro) might hold. Given that the board is quite dry and the opponent checked both the flop and turn, a bet here is unlikely to force a fold from a range that likely contains at least a 3 or an Ace, both of which are in the Big Blind's calling range. Checking is the most logical play to minimize losses and reach a showdown, as betting would effectively be a pure bluff that is unlikely to succeed.

The opponent has checked all three streets. With the Ace on the river, the board is K-K-3-7-A. My pocket twos give me two pair (Kings and Twos), which actually beats all unpaired non-Ace hands (like QJ, JT) since they only play the pair of Kings on the board. If I bet here, I am only getting called by better hands (an Ace, a higher pocket pair, or a slow-played King) and will force folds from the exact hands I already beat. Therefore, checking behind to realize my showdown value against their air is the most sensible and profitable play.

I am in the button position with Queen-Deuce offsuit, which is a very weak hand with poor playability. In a three-handed game, while ranges are wider on the button, Q-2o is still insufficient to enter the pot voluntarily. Folding preserves my stack to wait for a more favorable hand and position.

With A9 offsuit in the Small Blind after the Button folds, I have a strong enough hand to open-raise for value and fold equity against the Big Blind. Raising to 3 BBs (300 chips) will often win the pot immediately, and if called, I still hold a strong Ace.

I have Ad 3d in the Big Blind. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300. A3s is a strong hand in a blind-versus-blind scenario, possessing both high-card value and nut-flush potential. Since I will have post-flop position over the Small Blind, calling is a standard and effective play to realize my equity and keep the pot manageable while I evaluate the flop.

As the pre-flop aggressor on a dry, paired board (Q-Q-6), my range looks very strong and contains many Queens, high pocket pairs, and strong Aces. A small continuation bet will effectively fold out a lot of the Big Blind's missed overcards, suited connectors, and air hands. With Ace-high, I have decent equity, but I prefer to take the betting lead to win the pot immediately without facing pressure on later streets. A 1/3 pot bet of 200 chips is a highly efficient sizing here.

On a paired board like Qs-6c-Qd, the Small Blind will often lead out with a small continuation bet with a wide range of hands. My Ace-high (Ad 3d) has reasonable equity against this range and could still be the best hand. With 5:1 pot odds and position for the rest of the hand, calling is the most efficient play to see how the turn develops without committing too many chips yet.

After my small continuation bet is called on the flop, the opponent's range consists of medium pocket pairs (22-JJ), Qx, 6x, and various float hands like AK, AJ, or straight draws. The 7h on the turn is a relatively blank card, though it connects with some floats like 87 or 76. With Ace-high, I still have some showdown value against his missed overcards, but a double barrel is unlikely to fold out pocket pairs or any part of the board that currently beats me. Checking is the most prudent play to control the pot size, realize my equity, and evaluate my opponent's action.

Pro has checked the turn after betting the flop on a board with a pair of Queens. My Ace-high (Ad 3d) has decent showdown value in this blind-versus-blind situation. By checking back, I can see a free river card and keep the pot small, which is ideal since I likely have either the best hand or am significantly behind a hand like a slow-played Queen or a pocket pair. Betting wouldn't achieve much as better hands are unlikely to fold on this dry texture.

With Ace-high and a weak kicker on a paired board with a Jack on the river, my hand has marginal showdown value against missed floats or lower high cards, but it loses to any pair. Since I checked the turn for pot control and he checked behind, his range contains many medium pocket pairs, Jx, and better Ace-highs that won't necessarily fold to a bluff. Checking allows me to get to showdown cheaply against his air and safely fold if he makes a bet.

I have Ace-high in a blind-versus-blind pot. The board is paired (Queens) and relatively dry. Pro bet the flop but has checked both the turn and the river. This sequence often indicates a marginal hand or a missed draw. While my Ace-high has showdown value, it is unlikely to improve by betting, as better hands like small pocket pairs or a Jack are unlikely to fold on this board, and worse hands will not call. Checking back allows me to realize my showdown equity for free.

J7o is a weak offsuit hand, not strong enough to open raise even from the Button in 3-handed play. The optimal decision is to fold and wait for better starting hands to conserve chips and maximize my stack over the remaining hands.

In a blind vs blind situation, King-Seven offsuit is a strong enough hand to open for a raise. Pro has folded on the button, leaving only me and Flashlight in the big blind. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and potentially win it pre-flop.

Holding 8-3 suited in the big blind against a raise from the small blind is a clear fold. The hand has very little showdown value and poor playability. Defending here would be a mistake as it does not meet the necessary equity requirements for a call, and I should preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am on the button with Queen-Deuce offsuit. In a three-handed game, opening ranges are wider, but Q2o is still significantly below the threshold for a profitable raise or call, especially against opponents who have shown active play. Folding is the most disciplined and standard move here to preserve stack for better opportunities.

The hand 7s9h is weak and unsuited, offering poor potential for post-flop equity. Playing out of position against the big blind with such a hand is a losing strategy. Folding is the disciplined choice to conserve stack size.

I am holding 4c-3s, which is a very weak hand with minimal equity. Playing this hand from the button is not optimal, and given the early stage of the tournament, I should prioritize preserving my stack and waiting for better opportunities. Folding is the standard and disciplined choice here.

T4o is a very weak hand. Even in a blind vs blind situation where the Button has folded, it lacks the playability and equity to justify a call or a raise. The most prudent play is to fold and preserve the stack.
JTo is a standard open-raise from the button in a 3-handed game. By raising, I can steal the blinds immediately, and if called, I will have position throughout the hand post-flop.

I am in the Small Blind with Ah Qd, a premium starting hand in a 3-handed game. The Button (Pro) has opened with a standard raise to 250. To capitalize on the strength of my hand and to minimize the positional disadvantage I will face post-flop, I will 3-bet. A raise to 850 (approximately 3.4x the opening raise) is a standard size that builds a pot for value while putting pressure on the opener and discouraging the Big Blind from calling with marginal hands.

Holding Ten-Six offsuit in the Big Blind against a raise and a 3-bet is a clear fold. This hand has very poor equity against both opponents' ranges in a 3-bet pot, and calling would be burning chips. The smart move is to conserve my stack for a better spot.

We opened JTo from the button as a standard steal, but faced a large 3-bet from the Small Blind. Jack-Ten offsuit is not strong enough to call a 3.4x 3-bet, as it suffers from reverse implied odds and will miss the flop frequently. Folding is the most profitable decision here.

I am on the button with J3s. While suited hands have value, J3s is near the bottom of my potential opening range. Pro in the Big Blind has already shown aggression by 3-betting me in a previous hand (Hand #8). In a 3-handed game, I want to play a wide range from the button, but J3s is a bit too weak to comfortably raise and then fold to a 3-bet, or play post-flop out of position against a range that dominates my kicker. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

In the small blind with Queen-Deuce suited, after the button folded, I am heads-up against the big blind. The cost to complete is minimal (50 chips into a 150 chip pot), and the hand has decent playability, so calling is a standard strategic play.
The Small Blind limped. Holding Jack-Three suited, I have a marginal hand but can see a free flop from the Big Blind. Checking is the standard, low-risk play here.

I am in the small blind holding Queen-high (Qh-2h) on a board of 7c-Ks-Js. This board is very dry and favors the big blind's checking range, as they likely have many Kings and Jacks. With no pair and no realistic draw, my hand has virtually no showdown value. A bet here would be a pure bluff into a board that connects well with a calling range. Therefore, checking is the most prudent and strategic play to control the pot size and avoid unnecessarily bloating the pot with a weak hand.

I flopped middle pair with a weak kicker on a wet board (Ks Js 7c). Flashlight limped pre-flop and checked the flop, which often indicates weakness or a draw. However, betting with middle pair and a weak kicker on this board is thin and opens me up to being check-raised off my hand. Checking back controls the pot size and allows me to see the turn card safely.

I am in the small blind holding Queen-high (Qh-2h) on a board of 7c-Ks-Js-8s. This board has significant connectivity, and there is now a flush draw available. My hand has no showdown value and no draw. Betting here would be a pure bluff into a range that likely includes many Kings, Jacks, and potential draws, making it a highly unfavorable spot to build the pot. Checking is the most prudent decision to minimize losses and potentially see a free river card.

With middle pair and a weak kicker on a board with three spades and connected cards, my hand has minimal showdown value and no strong draws. Flashlight checking twice indicates weakness, but betting here could only get called by hands that beat me (a better Jack, a King, or someone trapping with a flush or a straight). Checking controls the pot size and allows me to see the river for free.

I hold Queen-high (Qh-2h) on a board of 7c-Ks-Js-8s-7h. The pot has been checked down through the flop and turn, indicating neither player has found a strong hand. Given the paired board and my complete lack of showdown value, betting would be an unnecessary bluff into a range that likely includes some pairs or missed draws that might call a small bet. Checking is the most prudent action to avoid inflating a pot I am unlikely to win.

We have two pair (Jacks and Sevens) with a King kicker. Flashlight checked three streets, indicating marginal showdown value or a missed draw. While we likely have the best hand, betting is unlikely to get called by worse hands, as weaker pairs will likely fold, and any better hand (a flush, a King, a better Jack, or trip Sevens) will call or raise. Checking behind ensures we get to showdown without risking extra chips, perfectly matching our hand's marginal showdown value.

I am on the button with K6 offsuit, which is a marginal but playable hand in a 3-handed game. Given I am in the best position and it is the first action, I will open-raise to 300 to attempt to steal the blinds and take initiative in the hand.
I have 72o, the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Facing a raise from the Button, I will easily fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Six offsuit. The Button (Flashlight) has raised to 300. In a three-handed game, Button opening ranges are typically quite wide. A6o is a strong enough hand to defend the Big Blind against a single raise, as it has significant raw equity and some showdown value. While I will be out of position for the rest of the hand, the pot odds (200 to call into a pot of 450) make calling a standard and profitable play here.

I have bottom pair with top kicker on a Jack-high board. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor (Flashlight), the standard play is to check to the raiser. This allows me to see how they react to the board and provides an opportunity to evaluate the strength of their continuation bet. My hand is too weak to lead out into but has enough value to potentially check-call one street depending on the sizing.

I have bottom pair on a relatively dry board. As the pre-flop aggressor, a continuation bet of about 40% of the pot (250) allows me to maintain initiative, deny equity to overcards, and see where I stand. Checking behind is a viable alternative to pot control, but betting is generally preferred to charge draws and realize my equity.

I have bottom pair with an Ace kicker on a Jack-Seven-Six board. Flashlight made a small continuation bet of 250 into a 650 pot. This sizing is relatively weak, often indicating a wide range of hands or a medium-strength hand like a marginal Jack, a middle pair, or even high-card air. Given the pot odds (calling 250 to win 900), I have more than enough equity and showdown value to continue. Folding here would be too tight, and raising would turn my hand into a bluff unnecessarily. Calling allows me to keep the pot manageable and reassess on the turn.

The turn card is the Jack of Clubs, which pairs the board. While this improves my hand to two pair (Jacks and Sixes), it is a neutral-to-bad card for my specific hand strength relative to the opponent's range, as any Jack in Flashlight's opening range now has trips. However, the pairing of the board also makes it less likely they have a Jack. Since I am out of position and called a bet on the flop, the standard play is to check to the aggressor. This allows me to control the size of the pot and see how Flashlight reacts to the board pairing. I still have some showdown value with my pair of sixes and Ace kicker if the hand stays small.

The board has paired Jacks, making it potentially dangerous. With my pair of 6s, I have showdown value but I am likely behind any hand that contains a Jack or a pocket pair higher than 6s. Since Flash checked, checking behind is the best move to maintain pot control and evaluate the river without inflating the pot unnecessarily with a marginal hand.

I have a pair of sixes with an Ace kicker on a board that has paired Jacks. My hand has significant showdown value in a three-handed game, especially since Flashlight checked the turn after I called their flop bet. Their check on the turn suggests they likely do not have a Jack or an extremely strong hand. By checking on the river, I am looking to get to a cheap showdown. My hand is strong enough to beat their missed draws (like 5-4 or overcards) but not strong enough to value bet, as most hands that would call a bet likely have me beaten (like a 7, an 8, or a 9). I will check and likely call a small bet or simply see the showdown if they check back.

I have a pair of sixes with a King kicker on a board of Jh-7d-6c-Jc-9d. This is a marginal hand that likely only beats total air or missed straight draws. The opponent checked the river, indicating they are likely giving up or waiting for a bluff. Betting here serves little purpose as I will likely only be called by better hands (any seven, any nine, or any pocket pair higher than sixes). Checking behind allows me to go to showdown and win against the specific range of hands the opponent is likely folding or checking with, while avoiding unnecessary risk of a check-raise.

7h 3h is a very weak hand, even though it's suited. Being on the button gives positional advantage, but the hand strength is too low to justify opening, especially early in the tournament where stealing with marginal hands isn't strictly necessary. Folding is the safest and most standard play.

I am in the Small Blind with Q4s. While I only need to call 50 more to see a flop, this is a very weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position against Flashlight in the Big Blind. Flashlight has been relatively active, and the 100/50/100 blind structure makes the decision close, but folding Q4o (offsuit) is standard and disciplined here to avoid marginal spots where I might be dominated or bluffed off the hand.

I am on the button with 42s. While suited, this hand is extremely weak for an opening range, even in a three-handed game. My stack is healthy at over 100 big blinds, and I don't see a compelling reason to play such a marginal hand from this position when the blinds (Flashlight and Pro) have shown willingness to defend or 3-bet in previous hands. Folding is the most disciplined play here.

6-2 offsuit is an extremely weak hand with very little equity. Being out of position against the Big Blind makes playing this hand even less desirable. I will fold to minimize losses and wait for a stronger starting hand.

The hand Td-2d is very weak and lacks sufficient equity to open from the button, even considering the positional advantage. Folding is the disciplined play to conserve my chip stack for better opportunities.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Three offsuit, a very weak hand. Since the Button folded, I am first to act. Playing this hand out of position against the Big Blind is unprofitable. Folding is the optimal decision.

KJo is a strong starting hand in 3-handed play. Since I am on the button and first to act, open-raising is the standard play to either steal the blinds or build a pot in position. Raising to 2.5x is a standard sizing.

I am in the Small Blind with 9d 5s, which is a very weak hand. Pro has opened with a 2.5x raise from the Button. In this position, calling with such a wide range of junk hands is a losing strategy over the long run, especially since I would be out of position for the remainder of the hand. Folding is the standard and most prudent play here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

7c 2c is one of the weakest possible hands in Texas Hold'em. Despite the decent pot odds offered, playing such a weak hand out of position against an open raise is consistently negative expected value. It is best to fold, preserve the stack, and wait for a more playable hand.

I am on the button with J7 offsuit. This is a marginal hand, but in a three-handed game, playing the button is important for positional advantage. However, J7o is generally below the standard opening range for the button. Looking at the table dynamics, Flashlight and Pro have been active. Since the pot is small and I'm facing a standard blind call of 50 more to complete (if it were a limp) or just acting first, I have to decide whether to open or fold. In this specific tournament setup, J7o is a bit too weak to raise profitably from the button against two capable opponents. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my stack.

I am in the Small Blind and the Button has folded. I have KJo, which is a strong hand in a heads-up situation against the Big Blind. Raising to 300 allows me to take initiative in the hand, capitalize on my card advantage, and potentially win the pot pre-flop or control the betting structure. Limping is too passive here.
Q3o is a very weak, disconnected offsuit hand. Facing a 3x open raise from the Small Blind, defending from the Big Blind is generally unprofitable. The best play is to fold and preserve my stack.

I am on the button with a pocket pair of fives, which is a strong starting hand in a three-handed game. Opening with a raise is the optimal strategy to either steal the blinds or take the initiative in the hand with a good equity advantage. A standard raise to 300 chips is appropriate to exert pressure on the blinds.
63 offsuit is a very weak hand with extremely low equity and playability. Facing a raise from the button, it is a clear fold from the small blind.

I am in the Big Blind with Td 6h. The button (Flashlight) has raised to 300 (3x). Pro in the Small Blind has folded. While I am getting roughly 3:1 odds to call (200 to win a total pot of 650), T6o is a very weak hand that realizations equity poorly out of position. Against a standard button opening range, this hand is a fold. I prefer to wait for better spots rather than defending with junk hands out of position.

I am on the Button with 8s 6d, which is an offsuit gapper. This is a very weak starting hand, not suited and not closely connected enough to justify an open raise or a call. Folding is the optimal play here.
K8o is a strong hand to play heads-up from the small blind when the button folds. Raising to 300 is a standard play to exert pressure on the big blind and potentially win the pot immediately.

Holding 9d 7h in the big blind against a raise from the small blind. While the pot odds are reasonable, 9-7 offsuit is a weak hand to play out of position against an aggressive small blind. I prefer to preserve my stack and wait for a more favorable situation.

Holding QTo on the button in a three-handed game is a clear opening situation. This hand has good playability and is well ahead of the average range of the blinds. I will raise to 300 to exert positional pressure and build a pot with a solid semi-broadway hand.

I am in the small blind with a weak, offsuit 5-4 hand. Playing this hand out of position against a button raise is negative expected value. Folding is the disciplined play here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Queen offsuit, a very strong starting hand. The Button (Flash) opened to 3 big blinds, which typically represents a wide range of hands in an attempt to steal the blinds. To extract value, take control of the hand, and mitigate my positional disadvantage, 3-betting is the optimal play. A standard out-of-position 3-bet sizing is around 3 to 4 times the initial raise, so I will raise to 1000.

I opened with QTo on the button, which is a standard play in a three-handed game. However, Pro's 3-bet to 1000 is a significant sizing (3.3x my raise). QTo is a marginal hand that is easily dominated by Pro's 3-betting range, which likely includes stronger Broadway hands (AQ, KQ) and medium-to-high pairs. While the positional advantage is helpful, calling 700 more with a non-suited hand that doesn't play particularly well in 3-bet pots is risky. Given the deep stacks and the early stage of the tournament (hand 18 of 150), I choose to fold and wait for a more favorable spot rather than committing more chips to a potentially dominated position.

I am on the button with Ace-Ten offsuit in a three-handed game. This is a strong starting hand that plays well in position. The blinds are 50/100, and opening for a raise is the standard play to take initiative and potentially take down the blinds or play the hand in position against one or both opponents. I will raise to 300 to build the pot and apply pressure.

Facing a 3x raise from the Button, King-Ten offsuit is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position from the Small Blind. Calling invites a potential squeeze from the Big Blind and leaves us playing out of position post-flop with a hand susceptible to domination. Folding is the safest and most standard play here.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Five offsuit. Flashlight raised from the Button to 300. Although the price to call is 200 into a 450 pot, Ten-Five offsuit is a very weak hand that performs poorly out of position against a Button range. It's better to fold and wait for a stronger hand rather than defending with such a marginal holding.

Holding 2d 5s, which is an extremely weak, unsuited, and disconnected hand. Acting first pre-flop (Button in 3-handed play), the only mathematically sound decision is to fold and save my chips for a better starting hand.

I have been dealt Pocket Aces (Ac Ah) in the Small Blind. The button (Pro) has folded. This is the strongest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. My objective is to build a large pot while balancing my range. A standard open-raise from the small blind is typically 3x the big blind. I will raise to 300 to extract value from Flashlight (the Big Blind) and to protect my equity.

I hold a premium hand, Ace-King offsuit, and am in the big blind against an open from the small blind. Given the strength of my cards, I want to take the initiative and apply pressure to maximize value, as this hand performs well against a wide opening range. A 3-bet to 900 is an appropriate sizing to either take down the pot immediately or build a larger pot while I have the lead.

I have been dealt Pocket Aces, the strongest possible starting hand. After my initial open to 300, Flashlight 3-bet to 900 from the Big Blind. I will now 4-bet to 2300 to extract maximum value and potentially induce a shove from Flashlight, as I want to get as much money into the pot as possible while I have a massive equity advantage.

My opponent has 4-bet to 2300, indicating significant strength. However, Ace-King offsuit is a premium hand that I cannot fold here, especially with the positional advantage of being in the big blind. While I could consider a 5-bet shove to put maximum pressure on the opponent, calling the 2300 total allows me to see a flop, realize my equity, and control the pot size while potentially inducing bluffs from the opponent on later streets.

I have Pocket Aces, the strongest overpair, on a dry 9-high board with a backdoor flush draw. The pot is already 4600 and the remaining effective stack is 6400 (SPR of 1.4). Given Flashlight called a 4-bet pre-flop, their range likely includes high pocket pairs (KK-TT) and strong broadway cards like AK/AQ. I will lead with a continuation bet of approximately 1/3 pot (1600) to extract value from their pocket pairs and AK/AQ, while setting up an easy shove on the turn.

I am facing a continuation bet of 1600 into a pot of 4600. While I have missed the flop with Ace-King, I have significant equity potential against a range that includes unpaired high cards or medium pairs.
