I am in the Small Blind with an extremely weak and unconnected offsuit hand (T5o). With the Button folding, I am facing the Big Blind. There is no reason to invest any chips with this trash hand, so the optimal play is to simply fold.

I am on the Button with AK suited, which is a premium starting hand. A standard raise to 2.5x (250) will build the pot, take control of the hand, and potentially isolate the blinds. If the short-stacked Big Blind shoves, I have a strong enough hand to call.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Four of hearts. The button (Pro) has opened for a 2.5x raise. While the hand is suited, it is quite weak to play out of position against a button open, especially with the Big Blind (Flashlight) sitting on a relatively short stack of 30BB, which makes them prone to shoving. To preserve my chip lead and avoid a difficult post-flop situation out of position, I will choose to fold.

Holding five-nine offsuit in the big blind against a button open is a clear fold. The hand has poor equity and playability, and despite the decent pot odds, it is not worth investing chips with such a weak holding. Preserving my stack for better opportunities is the priority.

I am holding 72s on the button. While I have the chip lead and position, 72s is one of the weakest hands in poker. Flashlight is relatively short-stacked and might look to shove or defend aggressively from the small blind. There is no reason to risk chips with such a low-equity hand when I can wait for better spots to utilize my stack advantage.

With the button having folded, I am in the small blind against the big blind. Queen-Seven offsuit is a decent hand for a heads-up situation, and raising provides a strong opportunity to take the initiative and potentially win the pot pre-flop.

Flashlight opens to 3x the big blind from the Small Blind. Although this is a blind-versus-blind situation and he likely has a wide stealing range, A3o is a weak offsuit Ace that plays poorly post-flop. Calling a 3x raise with this hand can lead to significant reverse implied odds if an Ace flops, as we will often be out-kicked. Furthermore, 3-betting against his ~27.5 BB stack size would be awkward, as we cannot comfortably call a 4-bet shove. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve our stack.

As the button in a three-handed game, I need to be more aggressive to increase my short stack. 10d-7d is a suited hand with some playability, making it a reasonable candidate for an opening raise from the button to attempt to steal the blinds. A raise to 250 is a standard 2.5x BB opening size.
7c 2c is an extremely weak hand. Facing a raise from the button, the optimal play from the small blind is to fold.

I am in the Big Blind with 95s. Flashlight, the short stack on the button, has raised to 250. I need to call 150 more to see the flop, which gives me pot odds of approximately 2.6:1. Given my massive chip lead and the suited nature of my hand, this is a standard defend against a button open. I will call and see if I can improve on the flop.

I have defended the big blind with 9c 5c and the flop is Qh Jc Kc. This board is extremely coordinated and hits a Button opening range very hard. I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a 10) and a backdoor flush draw. However, any 10 that completes my straight also completes a higher straight for any hand containing an Ace. Given the board texture and my position, checking is the only standard play here. I will see how Flashlight reacts before deciding whether to continue.

The flop is highly coordinated with King, Queen, and Jack, which hits a Big Blind's calling range very hard. My ten-high hand only has a gutshot straight draw, making it difficult to win by betting. Checking behind is the most prudent strategy to control the pot size and realize my equity for free if possible.

The board is extremely coordinated (Qh Jc Kc Jh), and my 9-high has almost no showdown value. However, the gutshot straight draw (needing a 10) is also weak because any Ace makes a higher straight. Since Flashlight checked back the flop, they might be weak, but this board texture hits a Button opening range very hard. I will check to see if Flashlight checks behind again, which allows me a free look at the river, or if they decide to bet, in which case I will likely fold.

With the turn having been checked to me, I have an open-ended straight draw on a paired board (J-J-Q-K). Betting into a paired board is risky as my opponent could easily hold a Jack, giving them trips or a full house. Checking behind is the most prudent strategic decision here to control the pot size and realize my equity for free, avoiding a potential check-raise that would force me to fold my equity.

On the river, the board is Qh Jc Kc Jh Ac. I held 9c 5c, and with the Jc, Kc, and Ac on the board, I have completed a 9-high flush. While the board is paired (JJ) and a straight is possible for anyone with a Ten, my flush is a very strong hand in this spot. Flashlight checked the flop and turn, suggesting they might not have a monster like a Jack or a high pair, or they are playing a draw/weak Ace. I will bet 300 into the 550 pot to extract value from a straight (any 10), an Ace, or even a King, while remaining cautious if raised due to the possibility of a full house.

The board shows Q-J-K-J-A. With my 10 in hand, I have a straight (10-J-Q-K-A). The board is highly connected, and any player with a 10 shares this straight. While it is possible for the opponent to have a full house (if they hold Q-Q, K-K, A-A, or J-x), folding a straight on a board that presents it to everyone is incorrect, especially for such a small bet relative to the pot size. Calling is the only logical move to secure my share of the pot.

Holding 6h 9d on the button, my hand is too weak to justify an open raise or call. The standard play here is to simply fold and wait for a better spot, especially with Flashlight having a relatively short stack in the Big Blind and Flash having a deep stack in the Small Blind.

I am in the Small Blind with 3c 7h, a very weak hand. The Button has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind, Flashlight, who has a relatively short stack of 2500 chips (25 BBs). While I only need to call 50 more to see a flop, 37o plays very poorly out of position and has little post-flop potential. It is better to fold and wait for a stronger hand than to invest chips in a marginal situation.

I am on the button with 82o, which is one of the weakest hands in Texas Hold'em. While being on the button offers a positional advantage, this hand lacks both high-card value and connectivity. Flashlight (SB) is short-stacked with 26 BBs, making them more likely to play shove-or-fold, which makes a bluff with such a weak hand risky. There is no reason to enter the pot with this hand.

I am the Small Blind holding 8s 4s, a very weak hand. With a short stack of 2600, I need to be selective about the pots I enter. Since the Button folded, I could complete the small blind, but with such a weak hand, it is statistically better to fold and preserve my chips rather than invest more against a larger stack.

I am on the button with a suited connector (9d 8d). This is a strong position to open the action, and with a stack of 2600 (26 big blinds), I need to be aggressive to maintain my tournament life and build my stack. A raise to 300 is my standard opening size in this tournament, which should be sufficient to isolate the blinds or take down the pot immediately.
K5o is too weak to play from the small blind against a button open, especially with the big blind yet to act. Folding is the standard play.

I am in the Big Blind with 3s 6c, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Flashlight, who is on a short stack of 2,300, has raised to 300 from the button. While I only need to call 200 more into a pot of 450, 36o has extremely poor equity and playability post-flop, especially out of position. Given my chip lead, there is no reason to defend with trash against a desperate short stack's open.
I am on the button with 3h6s, which is an extremely weak, unsuited, disconnected hand. There is no strategic merit to playing this hand, so folding is the obvious decision.

I am in the Small Blind with Qd 8d, a strong semi-connected suited hand. The action has folded to me. The Big Blind, Flashlight, is short-stacked with only 2650 chips (26.5 BBs). Since the button folded, I have a choice to complete the blind or raise. Given the short stack of the Big Blind, a raise to 300 is standard to put pressure on them, but since I only need to call 50 to see a flop, and I want to play a pot with a hand that has good post-flop potential, I will raise to 300 to try and take the blinds or play a heads-up pot where I have the initiative.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Jack offsuit, which is a strong hand against a Small Blind open. With 26.5 big blinds in my stack, folding is out of the question, and calling is a standard approach to keep the pot manageable and see a flop with a hand that holds decent equity against a wide opening range.

On a flop of 5c-9h-8s, I have middle pair (8s) with a Queen kicker. This is a solid but vulnerable hand on a coordinated board with straight possibilities (67, JT, T7). I was the pre-flop aggressor. Flashlight is relatively short-stacked (24.5 BBs). A continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) is a standard play here to maintain the initiative, extract value from draws or smaller pairs (like 5x or small pocket pairs), and protect my hand against overcards like Ace-high or King-high that might have missed the flop. If Flashlight raises, I will have to re-evaluate based on the size, but a bet allows me to define the strength of his range.

The opponent has bet 300 into a 600 pot on a 5-9-8 rainbow board. With King-Jack offsuit, I have two overcards to the board. While I do not have a pair, the pot odds are attractive (3:1), and my hand has potential to improve on the turn. Folding seems too passive given the stack-to-pot ratio, so I will call the bet to see a turn card and evaluate further.

I have middle pair (8s) with a Queen kicker. The turn 2d is a total blank. After betting the flop and being called, I want to control the size of the pot. Flashlight's calling range includes 9x, 8x, or straight draws (67, JT). By checking, I can evaluate Flashlight's action and potentially get to a cheap showdown or induce a bluff from missed draws on the river. Betting again might commit me against a short stack who has a 9.

The turn has brought the 2d, which is a brick and doesn't improve my King-high hand. Flash has checked the turn after betting the flop. Given I have no pair and limited showdown value, betting as a bluff is risky and likely inefficient given the stack-to-pot ratio. Checking behind is the most prudent play, allowing me to take a free river card and re-evaluate, while keeping the pot manageable.

I have middle pair (8s) with a Queen kicker. The board paired on the river with a deuce, which is unlikely to have helped Flashlight's range given the previous action. After I checked the turn and Flashlight checked behind, it's likely they have a marginal hand or a missed draw. Betting now is unlikely to get value from worse hands, as most hands that call me will likely have a 9 or better. Checking is the most prudent way to get to showdown or induce a bluff from missed straight draws like JT, T7, or 67.

I am in the Big Blind with King-high on a board of 5-9-8-2-2. My opponent checked the turn and the river, indicating they may have missed or are trapping. With only King-high, I have very limited showdown value and am losing to any pair in my opponent's range. Betting is unlikely to succeed as a bluff because any pair will call, and I cannot improve my hand. Checking behind is the most standard, risk-averse play to reach showdown.

I am on the button with Q9o. In a 3-handed game, this is a standard opening hand. My stack is very healthy (177 BBs), while Flashlight is relatively short (21 BBs). I'll raise to 300 to put pressure on the blinds and potentially take the pot pre-flop or play in position against one of them.

I am in the small blind with JhTh, a strong suited connector that performs well in pots. The pot is currently 450, and I need to call 250 to proceed. With a stack of 2100, calling allows me to see a flop with a decent hand while maintaining my stack integrity. Folding would be too passive, and 3-betting would commit too much of my stack out of position. Therefore, calling is the most balanced and strategic move.

I have A9s in the Big Blind facing a Button open and a Small Blind call. Getting great pot odds (200 to win 700) to close the action pre-flop, calling is a very profitable play. A9s plays well multi-way due to its flush and top-pair potential. While squeezing is an option, flatting is lower variance and keeps the pot manageable, especially with the Small Blind having a relatively short stack.

The board of Ah-6d-6s is extremely dry and heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor, Flash, who likely holds an Ace in their range. With Jh-Th, I have no pair, no draw, and effectively zero showdown value. Betting here would be a low-percentage bluff into a range that likely connects well with this board. Checking is the most strategic option to maintain control and see how the aggressor reacts.

With top pair and a mediocre kicker on a dry, paired board (Ah 6d 6s), there is no need to donk bet into the pre-flop aggressor and the short-stacked Small Blind. Checking to the raiser is standard here, allowing them to c-bet their bluffs or weaker hands. My hand is strong enough to call a bet but not strong enough to start building a huge pot out of position.

On a dry board of Ace-high with a pair of sixes, I have limited value with Queen-high. While I was the pre-flop aggressor and a continuation bet could represent an Ace, checking back allows me to control the pot size and see a free turn card. Flashlight is quite short-stacked and might feel compelled to shove over a bet, and Pro is a solid player who could be trapping with an Ace or a Six. Checking back maintains my position and gives me a chance to improve or evaluate the turn based on their actions.

The board is A-A-6-6, which is an extremely dry board that pairs the Ace and the Six. With Jh-Th, I have no pair and no realistic showdown value. Since I have checked the flop and the turn, and there has been no betting from my opponents, the pot remains relatively small at 900 chips. Given my stack size of 1850 and the presence of two deeper-stacked opponents who have also checked, attempting a bluff here is high-risk and low-reward. The most prudent and balanced strategy is to continue checking, hoping to either see a cheap river card or avoid committing more chips with a weak holding.

The turn brings another Ace, giving me Aces full of Sixes, which is virtually the nuts. Since both opponents checked the flop, it's possible neither has an Ace, but they might have pocket pairs, a Six, or have picked up a spade flush draw. Checking again might induce a bluff from the button, but it also risks the turn checking through, missing a street of value. I'll make a small-to-medium value bet to start building the pot, targeting sticky pocket pairs and draws that might want to see a river.

The board has double-paired (Aces and Sixes). Pro has led out for 400 into a 900 pot after a check-around on the flop. With only Queen-high and no real draw, I am likely beat by any pocket pair, any Ace, or any Six. Even if Pro is bluffing, Flashlight is still to act behind me and is short-stacked, which could lead to a shove. There is no reason to commit more chips with a marginal hand in this spot.

The board is paired with Aces and Sixes, and I hold only Jack-high with no pair or draw. My opponent has shown aggression on the turn after checking the flop, which could indicate a small pair or a weak Ace that is already well ahead of my hand. There is no reasonable path to winning this pot with my current holding, and calling 400 into a 1300 pot without any equity would be a significant mistake. Folding is the only logical and disciplined decision.

With Q3 offsuit in the button position and a short stack of 18.5 big blinds, this is not a strong enough hand to justify investing chips. It is better to conserve my remaining stack for a more favorable situation or a hand with better equity, as a blind steal attempt here is high-risk with very low reward potential against the active blinds.

I have 72 offsuit in the small blind, which is statistically the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Despite the small cost to complete, playing this hand out of position against the big blind is a losing proposition. Folding is the only sensible choice.

With 52o on the button, this hand is extremely weak and not worth opening, especially considering the big blind has a short stack that could easily shove over a raise. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Small Blind with 3d 6d. The Button has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind, Flashlight. Flashlight is currently very short-stacked with only 1,750 chips (17.5 BB). While 3d 6d is suited, it is a very weak hand. If I raise, I risk a shove from a desperate short stack. If I call, I give him a free look at the flop or the chance to shove over my limp. Given my substantial chip lead over both opponents, there is no need to play high-variance pots with marginal hands against a short stack. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to apply pressure.

I have K4s on the button with a 174bb stack. The Small Blind (Flashlight) is very short-stacked with only 18.5bb remaining. Given the position and the potential to pressure the short stack, a raise is standard here to try and steal the blinds or play a pot against a weakened opponent. A standard 2.5x to 3x raise should be effective.

6d 5h is a very weak hand with poor equity, especially playing out of position against a button raise. With my stack significantly depleted to 1850 chips, I need to conserve my remaining resources for a more favorable situation. Folding is the correct disciplined play.

Pocket sevens are a solid hand to defend from the big blind against a button open. With deep effective stacks, set-mining is very profitable, and 77 also has standalone value on many runouts. Calling keeps the pot manageable out of position.

Out of position on a K-high board with a flush draw, my pocket sevens are a marginal made hand. Checking to the pre-flop raiser is the standard line, allowing me to evaluate his continuation bet or realize equity if he checks back.

I have flopped top and bottom pair (Kings and Fours) on a K-8-4 board. This is a very strong hand in a three-way pot, especially heads-up against the Big Blind. Pro checked to me, and there are potential flush draws (spades) and gutshot straight draws available. I need to bet for value and to protect my hand. A bet of approximately 60% of the pot (400 into 650) is a standard size to get called by weaker Kings, draws, or pocket pairs like 99-QQ.

Out of position with third pair on a King-high board that features a flush draw and straight draw possibilities. Facing a continuation bet of over half the pot, my pocket sevens are likely behind or at risk of being outdrawn on later streets. Continuing here would be playing a guessing game out of position, so folding is the disciplined and most EV-preserving choice.

I am the button in a three-handed game with 18.5 big blinds, which makes me relatively short-stacked. A-5 offsuit is a strong enough hand to play in a short-handed format, especially from the button. Raising might commit too much of my limited stack, so calling the big blind to see a flop is a more prudent way to play, allowing me to evaluate the board before committing more chips.

With 94o in the small blind, this is a very weak hand with poor playability post-flop. Even though it's only 50 more chips to call, being out of position against a limper and the big blind makes it a clear fold. I want to save my chips for better spots.

Flashlight limped from the button and Pro folded. With 9s Tc, I have a decent but not premium hand. Given Flashlight's short stack (17.5 BB), raising might induce a shove that I don't necessarily want to call with this hand. Checking allows me to see a flop for free and preserve my chip lead.

I have 9s Tc on a board of As 8s 5h. Flashlight limped from the button and I checked the big blind. This board doesn't hit my hand at all, but I have a backdoor straight draw. Flashlight has a very short stack (1750 chips). There is no reason to bet here and risk a shove or bloat the pot when I have zero showdown value right now. I will check and see if I can get a free turn card.

I have flopped two pair (Aces and Fives) on an Ace-high board with a potential flush draw. This is a very strong hand, and I need to bet to build value and protect against draws. Betting 200 into the 250 pot provides a solid price to continue with my hand while keeping my opponent's range wide enough to call.

I have 9-high on an Ace-high board with very little equity. Flashlight, who is short-stacked, has bet 200 into a 250 pot. Since I have no pair, no flush draw, and only a very weak backdoor straight draw, there is no reason to continue in this hand. I will fold and preserve my chips.

Ten-Three offsuit is a very weak starting hand. Being on the button does not justify playing this unplayable hand, especially with a short-stacked big blind who might shove over a steal attempt. Folding is the only optimal play here.

I am in the Small Blind with 8s Th. The Button (Pro) has folded. The Big Blind (Flashlight) is short-stacked with only 1900 chips (19 BB). 8-10 offsuit is a reasonable hand to play heads-up against the Big Blind. I will call the additional 50 chips to see a flop, keeping the pot small and avoiding a potential shove from a short-stacked opponent while still staying in the hand with a decent starting range.
I am in the big blind and the small blind has completed the blind to match my 100. With Jack-Eight offsuit, I have no reason to raise, so checking is the standard play to see a free flop.

The flop is extremely dry (7h-2d-5s). Since Flashlight checked the big blind pre-flop, their range is wide and likely consists of many hands that missed this board entirely. I'll lead out with a small bet of 100 (half the pot) to try and take it down immediately. If Flashlight shows any resistance, I can comfortably fold given I have no made hand or significant draw.

I hold Jack-high on a dry 7-2-5 board with no draw or pair. My opponent's bet represents at least some piece of the board or a continuation bet, and I have no equity to justify calling a bet with such a weak hand. Folding is the disciplined play to preserve my remaining stack.

I am on the button with 8d 7h. While 87o is sometimes an opening hand in 3-handed play, Flashlight in the Small Blind is relatively short-stacked (1850 chips, or 18.5 BB) and may be looking for a spot to shove. Raising here commits chips to a pot where I might have to fold to a re-raise, and calling (limping) is generally a weak strategy from the button. Given my substantial chip lead, I will choose the conservative path and fold this marginal hand to wait for a better opportunity.

I am in the Small Blind and the Button (Flash) has folded. The pot currently contains 150, and I have already contributed 50 as the Small Blind. I need to call 50 to see the flop against the Big Blind (Pro). With 2c 9c, this is a reasonable hand to complete the small blind with, as the pot odds (50 to win 100) are favorable.

With 9h 7c in the big blind facing a limp from the small blind, who is short-stacked, I have a marginal hand. The best play is to check and take a free flop, avoiding bloating the pot out of position against a player who might be trapping or preparing to shove.

I missed the flop completely with 2c 9c on a Qs 4h 8s board. I have no pair and no strong draw, so there is no value in betting. Checking is the standard play here to keep the pot small and re-evaluate on the turn.

I have 9-high with practically no equity on a Q-4-8 flop. My opponent is short-stacked and checked to me. Taking a free card is the optimal play, as bluffing into a short stack with nothing is risky and unnecessary.

I am in the Small Blind with 9-high on a board of Qs 4h 8s Qh. My hand has no value and no real equity. The pot is small, and checking is the standard play to keep the pot size controlled and see if I can get to a cheap showdown or fold if the opponent decides to bet. There is no reason to initiate aggression here.

With 9-high on a paired board and no real draw, I have zero equity. Even though my opponent checked twice, bluffing a short stack can be risky since they might be trapping or inclined to call down light. Checking again takes a free river card without risking chips.

The board is paired (Queens and Eights) with a 4 kicker. My hand (9-high) has absolutely no showdown value against the Big Blind's range. I have already checked on the river, and since there is no bet to call, checking down is the standard and correct play to reach the showdown without risking further chips.

With 9-high on a double-paired Q-Q-8-8 board, my hand plays as Q-Q-8-8-9, meaning my 9 acts as the kicker. Flashlight has checked to me on all three streets. Given their short stack, they are very likely to call a river bet with any Ace-high, King-high, Jack-high, or Ten-high, all of which beat me. Bluffing into a short-stacked player in a small pot is unnecessary and risky. I will check behind and hope my 9 kicker is good enough to beat a counterfeited small pair or a weaker high-card.

I am in the button position with a weak hand, Jack-Five offsuit. With a short stack of 19 big blinds, I need to be selective with my starting hands to preserve my stack and look for better opportunities. Opening with this hand is unlikely to be profitable, and folding is the safest and most disciplined play.

With K3 suited in the Small Blind and the Button folding, I have a decent hand to attempt a steal. Raising takes the initiative, denies equity from random weak hands the Big Blind might hold, and sets up a continuation bet on favorable flops.

I am the chip leader and am currently in the big blind. Pro, in the small blind, has raised to 300 (a 3x raise). While I have position on Pro post-flop, my hand (6d 4h) is extremely weak and does not flop well. Even with favorable pot odds and position, defending with such a low-equity hand is generally a losing play against a standard opening range. Flashlight is the short stack, and there is no need for me to take unnecessary risks or play sub-optimal hands at this stage.

K4o is too weak of a hand to open, even from the button. Flashlight has a short stack (18 BBs) and might shove over any raise, and Flash has a deep stack. Folding is the standard and safest play here.

In a blind-vs-blind situation after the button folds, J9o is a solid hand to open-raise with. Flashlight is short-stacked with only 18 BBs, so a raise to 300 (3x the big blind) puts significant pressure on them. This action aims to either take down the blinds immediately or play a pot with the initiative against a vulnerable stack.

With 18 big blinds, holding Ace-Nine offsuit in the big blind against a raise from the small blind is a standard defending hand. I have decent equity against a wide range that the small blind might be opening, and folding would be too tight given the current stack size and pot odds. I will call to see the flop and evaluate from there.

I have flopped an open-ended straight draw on a board of 7-Q-T with my J9. My opponent, Flashlight, is very short-stacked with only 1,600 chips remaining (an SPR of 2.67). A continuation bet of 300 (half-pot) is effective here. It applies pressure to their marginal hands while keeping the pot manageable. If they shove, I have sufficient equity (approximately 32% to hit on the turn and nearly 50% by the river) to call given the pot odds I would be offered. Betting also allows me to take down the pot immediately if they missed the flop entirely.

I am in the big blind with Ace-High (Ah9d) on a board of 7s-Qc-Th. While I do not have a strong made hand, I have overcards and am facing a continuation bet of 300 into a 900 pot. The pot odds (1:4) are favorable enough to justify a call, especially given my stack-to-pot ratio and the likelihood that my opponent could be betting a wide range or a weaker hand. Folding would be too conservative here given the relative strength of Ace-high.

The turn card (7d) pairs the board, which is generally a neutral card. With an open-ended straight draw (8 outs) and Flashlight being very short-stacked (SPR of approximately 1), my goal is to realize my equity as cheaply as possible. If I check, I provide Flashlight with the opportunity to check behind, which would allow me to see the river for free. If Flashlight chooses to shove their remaining 1,300 chips into the 1,200 pot, I would need approximately 34% equity to call, but my straight draw only has about an 18% chance of completing on the final card. Therefore, checking is the most prudent play to potentially see a free river or fold to a shove if the price isn't right.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-High (Ah9d) on a board of 7s-Qc-Th-7d. My opponent (Flash) has checked to me on the turn. Since I have limited showdown value and minimal equity against a calling range, checking behind is the most disciplined and standard play. Betting here would likely only be called by better hands, and checking keeps the pot small while allowing me to see the river for free.

The river has completed a board of 7s-Qc-Th-7d-7c, giving the board three sevens. My hand remains Jack-high. Flashlight has a very short stack of 1,300 chips remaining in a pot of 1,200. At this point, my Jack-high is unlikely to win at showdown against Flashlight's calling range, which likely includes various Ace-high and King-high hands, or small pocket pairs that now play as a full house or better with the trips on board. However, bluffing into a short stack that is essentially pot-committed is risky, as they are likely to call with any Ace or even King-high. Checking is the most sensible play to see if we can win a cheap showdown or simply concede the pot if Flashlight bets.

The board shows three sevens (7s, 7d, 7c), which means everyone has at least a pair of sevens. My hand is Ace-High (A-9), which gives me a 7-7-7-A-9. This hand beats any lower kicker combinations the opponent might have (like 8-high, 6-high, etc.) and loses to any hand with a Queen, Ten, Jack, or King. Since the opponent checked the river, betting would only be a bluff that likely gets called by hands that beat me (like a Queen or a pair) or folds out hands I am already beating. Checking allows me to reach showdown and minimize losses while potentially winning against weaker hands.

I am on the button with 53o, which is a very weak starting hand even in a three-handed game. While I have a significant chip lead, there is no reason to play such a weak hand, especially with Flashlight being relatively short-stacked in the small blind and potentially looking for a spot to shove or defend aggressively. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

My hole cards are T4o, which is a weak starting hand with little potential to improve into a strong made hand against an opponent. While I have already invested 50 chips in the small blind and am getting decent pot odds to call, playing out of position with such a weak hand is generally unprofitable in the long run. I will fold and preserve my remaining stack.

I am the button with J-5 offsuit, which is a weak hand and not suitable for an opening raise or a call against two opponents. With a stack size of 2450 (24.5 BBs), it is better to preserve my stack and wait for a better opportunity to enter a pot, especially since I am not in the blinds and do not have to commit chips if I choose to fold.

With K4o in the small blind against the big blind who has a larger stack, playing out of position with a weak, disconnected hand is suboptimal. Given the short stack of the third player, there's no need to take unnecessary risks in a marginal spot. Folding is the most straightforward and conservative decision.

I am on the Button with 98o. The Big Blind is relatively short-stacked at 23.5 BBs, which makes him prone to 3-bet shoving, and the Small Blind is deep-stacked. Offsuit connectors like 98o play poorly against 3-bets and are marginal at best to open. Folding is the safest and most standard play here.

I am in the Small Blind with Qd4d. Flashlight, the Big Blind, is extremely short-stacked with only 2350 chips (23.5 BB). My hand is suited and has decent high-card value for a 3-handed game. Since the Button folded, it's just me and the short stack. I want to put pressure on Flashlight or simply take down the pot. Calling the extra 50 is an option, but raising to 300 is a standard play to attack the big blind. If Flashlight shoves, I can evaluate based on the odds, but a raise here is a strong play to isolate or win the blinds.

I am in the big blind with a pocket pair of sevens (7s, 7c). My opponent, Flash (Small Blind), has raised to 300. Given that I have 2350 chips (about 23 big blinds), a pocket pair is a strong holding in a blind-versus-blind scenario. While shoving is a potential option, calling is a solid, lower-variance play that keeps me in the hand to see a flop while maintaining a healthy stack-to-pot ratio. I will call the additional 200 chips to defend my blind.

I am the pre-flop aggressor in a battle of the blinds. The flop comes 9h-Td-Th, which is a paired board. While I haven't hit anything with my Qd-4d, this texture is often good for a continuation bet because it's difficult for the opponent to have a piece of it unless they have a Ten, a Nine, or a pocket pair. Flashlight is short-stacked, so I will put out a half-pot bet of 300 to see where I stand and try to take the pot down immediately. If they show any aggression (raise or shove), I will fold as my hand has very little equity.

The board of 9h-Td-Th is relatively wet, hitting ranges that include tens and nines. However, Flash is the Small Blind and a continuation bet here is standard with a wide range. I have a pair of 7s, which has some showdown value against bluffs or air. The bet is 300 into a pot of 900, giving me 4:1 pot odds. Calling is the most balanced approach to see if the turn improves my situation or allows me to re-evaluate without overcommitting, as folding might be too weak given the price.

The Turn card (8h) is quite dangerous as it connects the board further, potentially completing straights or flush draws. Flashlight has a very short stack remaining (1850) relative to the pot (1200), meaning they are likely to be sticky with any pair or draw. My Queen-high has no showdown value, but betting here risks getting shoved on by a desperate short stack. I will check to see how Flashlight responds and likely fold to any significant aggression, as my hand lacks equity on this texture.

I am in the Big Blind with a pair of sevens (7s, 7c) on a board of 9h-Td-Th-8h. I have an open-ended straight draw with a 6 or J. Flash (Small Blind) checked the turn. Checking behind is a strong, controlled play here, as it allows me to realize my equity for free and potentially hit my straight on the river, while also avoiding being check-raised on a board that hits many of Flash's potential holding ranges.

The river is a 2s, which doesn't change the board state significantly. I have Queen-high, which has almost no showdown value on this board (9h-Td-Th-8h-2s). Flashlight checked back the turn, suggesting they either have a marginal hand (like a 9 or an 8) or a complete miss. Given Flashlight is short-stacked (1850 chips), a bet might induce a desperate call from any pair or even Ace-high. Since I checked the turn, my range looks capped. I will check the river and hope for a cheap showdown, though I expect to lose most of the time. Betting here as a bluff against a short stack who might be looking for any reason to call is risky.

I have a pair of sevens (7s, 7c) on a board of 9h-Td-Th-8h-2s, resulting in two pair (tens and sevens). Given that the board is quite connected and Flash (the Small Blind) has checked the turn and river after betting the flop, their range likely contains many hands that beat my two pair, such as any ten, nine, or eight. My hand has very marginal showdown value, and betting would be risky as I would likely only be called by hands that beat me. Checking is the most prudent decision to see the showdown.

I am on the button with 73 suited. While suited, this is a very weak hand to open with in a 3-handed game, especially since the small blind (Flashlight) is relatively short-stacked at 30BBs and might be looking for a spot to shove. Given my significant chip lead, I can afford to wait for a stronger hand to apply pressure rather than risking chips with a bottom-tier starting hand.

With Flash folding on the button, I am in the Small Blind facing the Big Blind (Pro). My hand, Ace-Six offsuit, is a strong enough holding to potentially take down the pot pre-flop in a blind-versus-blind scenario. Given my shorter stack compared to the Big Blind, raising is the optimal play to pressure the opponent and attempt to steal the blinds. A raise to 250 is standard to force a fold or set up a continuation bet if necessary.

I am in the Big Blind with Q5o facing a raise from the Small Blind. Q5o is a weak, disconnected, offsuit hand that plays very poorly post-flop. Even with the discount from being in the big blind and getting decent pot odds, defending with this hand is unprofitable. Furthermore, the small blind has a short stack (2800), meaning implied odds are low and they might just be looking to shove over any aggression. Folding is the standard and correct play.

I am in the button position with a very weak hand, Queen-Seven offsuit. My stack is relatively short (3150) compared to my opponents, and I need to be selective with my starting hands to find a better spot to double up. Raising with this hand is unlikely to be profitable given the strength of the cards, and calling is not an option as I am the first to act. Therefore, folding is the most prudent strategic decision to preserve my remaining stack for a stronger holding.

With the action folded to me in the Small Blind, A2o is a reasonable hand to attempt a steal against the Big Blind. I will raise to 3 big blinds to put pressure on Flash and hopefully take down the pot uncontested, or at least take the betting lead if called.

I am holding 2d 4s, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Although I would have position on the Small Blind (Pro) for the rest of the hand, this hand has very little equity and does not flop well. Even with 2:1 pot odds, defending the big blind with 4-high offsuit is generally a losing play against a standard steal attempt. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.
J3o is a very weak starting hand with no coordination and poor high card strength. From the button in a 3-handed game, the standard and most profitable play is simply to fold.

I am in the Small Blind with QsJh. The Button (Pro) has folded. Flashlight is in the Big Blind with a very short stack of 3,050. I have a strong hand for heads-up play against a wide big blind range. Given Flashlight's short stack, I want to apply pressure. A standard raise is 3x the big blind, which is 300.

My hand (Ten-Four offsuit) is very weak and lacks playability. Even though I am in the Big Blind and getting decent pot odds, I am out of position against an aggressive player (Flash). Given my short stack size, it is better to preserve my remaining chips for a stronger opportunity than to defend such a marginal hand.

I am on the button with Jack-Six offsuit. While it is a three-handed game, this hand is generally too weak to open, especially with Flashlight in the small blind holding a stack of 30 big blinds which makes them prone to 3-bet shoving or calling and playing out of position. I have been active in recent orbits and can afford to wait for a better spot to maintain my chip lead.

I am in the small blind with pocket eights, a strong starting hand in a heads-up situation. With a stack of 3000 chips (30 big blinds), I have sufficient depth to play aggressively. Just calling would allow the big blind to see a cheap flop, so I will raise to 300 to exert pressure and take initiative in the pot.

I am in the Big Blind with a very weak hand, Ten-Four offsuit. The Small Blind raised to 3x the big blind. I have no strong equity or playability to justify calling or 3-betting, so the optimal and standard play is to fold.

I am on the button with [9c, Js], a marginal hand. With a stack of 3150 and blinds at 50/100, I should look to be aggressive to steal the blinds and maintain my tournament life. Raising to 300 is a standard open-raise size to apply pressure and potentially win the pot pre-flop or take initiative into the flop.

KQs is a premium hand, especially against a Button open. However, the Button has a relatively short stack (about 30 big blinds). If I 3-bet, I might face a 4-bet jam, putting me in a tough spot where I might have to fold a hand with great playability. Calling guarantees seeing a flop with a hand that flops very well, while keeping the pot manageable out of position.

I am in the Big Blind with 6d 4d. The Button (Flashlight) raised to 3x and the Small Blind (Pro) called. I am getting excellent pot odds to call (200 to win a total pot of 900, requiring about 22% equity). My hand is suited and has some connectivity, making it a viable candidate for a multi-way pot. Additionally, I have a significant chip lead, and Flashlight is relatively short-stacked, which gives me flexibility post-flop. Calling is the most efficient play to see a flop cheaply.

I flopped top pair with a very strong kicker on a wet board (flush and straight draws available). Being out of position in a multi-way pot, the standard and most prudent play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor (Flashlight). This allows me to potentially check-raise for value and protection, or check-call to keep the pot manageable, depending on the sizing and the action from the Big Blind.

I have middle pair (sixes) with a weak kicker on a coordinated board of Qh-6s-5s. The Small Blind (Pro) has checked. As I am in the Big Blind and the pre-flop raiser (Flashlight) is still to act behind me, checking is the most standard and prudent play. My hand has some showdown value but is not strong enough to lead out for value or as a bluff into two opponents. Checking allows me to see how the Button reacts and control the size of the pot.

The flop is [Qh, 6s, 5s]. I hold [9c, Js] and have essentially no equity (no pair, no flush draw, and very limited straight potential). With two opponents who have already checked to me, betting is a high-risk bluff that will likely be called by any Queen or flush draw. Since I am in position, checking back allows me to see the turn card for free while preserving my stack. Checking is the most disciplined and strategic play here given the board texture and my lack of a made hand or strong draw.

The turn brings a third spade, completing flush draws. I hold top pair with a good kicker but do not have a spade in my hand. Being out of position in a multi-way pot, checking is the safest and most standard option. It allows me to control the size of the pot and evaluate the actions of my opponents. Betting here could lead to being called or raised by flushes or strong draws with high spades, putting me in a difficult spot.

On the turn, the 9 of spades completes a possible flush and several straight draws. I have middle pair (sixes) with a weak kicker. Given that the flop was checked around and the Small Blind has checked again, my hand still has some showdown value but is not strong enough to bet for value into two opponents, especially with the board becoming more coordinated. Checking is the most prudent move to control the pot size and see how the Button (the pre-flop aggressor) reacts.

I have a pair of Nines (9c) on a board of Qh, 6s, 5s, 9s. While I have a pair, the board is dangerous with a Queen and a potential flush draw. Both opponents checked the flop and the turn, indicating they likely don't have a very strong hand, but betting into two opponents risks getting raised or called by a better hand. Checking allows me to maintain pot control, preserve my stack of 2850, and potentially get to a cheap showdown.

With top pair and a King kicker, my hand is almost certainly the best after the flop and turn checked through multi-way. The passive action heavily discounts any flushes or strong made hands from my opponents. The river is a total blank. I need to bet for value to get called by worse pairs, such as a pair of nines, or underpairs like TT or JJ that checked down. A sizing of roughly half-pot is ideal to extract value without scaring off marginal bluff-catchers.

I am in the Big Blind with fourth pair (sixes) on a board of Qh-6s-5s-9s-2d. The action has been checked around on both the flop and the turn, which strongly suggests that none of the players hold a premium hand like a flush, a set, or top pair. On the river, the Small Blind (Pro) has led out for 400 into a pot of 900. This small bet often represents a thin value bet with a marginal hand (like a 9 or a 5) or a specialized bluff. I am receiving pot odds of 4.25-to-1 (calling 400 to win a total pot of 1700), meaning I only need to be right about 19% of the time to break even. Given the passivity of the previous streets and the size of the bet, my pair of sixes has enough showdown value to justify a call, even with the Button still to act behind me, as his checks also capped his range.

The river board [Qh, 6s, 5s, 9s, 2d] is highly coordinated, completing potential flush and straight draws. Facing a lead bet from Pro and a call from Flash, my pair of Nines is extremely vulnerable and highly unlikely to be the best hand. With two opponents showing strength on this texture, calling to see a showdown would be a negative-expectation play. Folding is the correct strategic decision to minimize losses and preserve my remaining stack.

I am on the Button with a solid middle pocket pair (8d, 8s). Opening with a raise is standard here to steal the blinds or build a pot with a strong hand in position. Given the stack depths, particularly the Big Blind's shorter stack (27.5 bb), a raise to 2.5x (250 chips) provides a good risk-to-reward ratio.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Six offsuit. The Button (Pro) has opened for a standard raise to 2.5x the big blind. Jack-Six is a very weak hand to play out of position, and the Big Blind (Flashlight) is relatively short-stacked, which increases the likelihood of a three-bet shove. To maintain my chip lead and wait for a more profitable spot, I will fold.

I hold a strong pocket pair (Ten of Clubs, Ten of Diamonds). Against a raise from the button, pocket tens are strong enough to warrant a 3-bet to take the initiative, narrow the field, and build a pot. Raising to 850 is a standard 3-bet sizing that allows me to put pressure on my opponent while maintaining control of the pot given my current stack size of 2750.

Flashlight 3-bet from the Big Blind, leaving himself with 2000 chips (total starting stack of 2850, or 28.5 bb). With 8d 8s on the Button, calling to play an SPR 1 pot post-flop is difficult, as overcards will frequently hit the board, potentially forcing me to over-fold the best hand. A 4-bet shove applies maximum pressure, denies equity to any of his bluffs or semi-bluffs, and guarantees I realize my full equity against his calling range, which contains many combinations of overcards (like AK/AQ) where I am a slight favorite. Given his short stack, putting him all-in is the most profitable play.

I am facing a 4-bet from the button after my initial 3-bet with pocket tens. While this indicates strength, my stack is now pot-committed, and the pot odds are too favorable to fold. With 2000 chips remaining to call into a pot of 3750, I have the necessary equity to continue against a standard 4-betting range. Folding would be too passive given the investment, so I must commit my remaining stack.

I am on the button with 6s 9d, which is a weak offsuit hand. While I am the chip leader, this hand doesn't warrant a raise in a three-handed game against active opponents, especially since I'll be out of position relative to the blinds in many post-flop scenarios or facing a potential 3-bet. Folding is the most disciplined play here.

With Flash folding on the button, I am in the small blind against the big blind with Queen-five offsuit. While it is a weak hand, completing the blind (calling the 50) is a standard play in a blind-vs-blind scenario to see a flop cheaply against the big blind's wide range.

I hold 8c 7d offsuit in the Big Blind and the Small Blind has just limped. This hand is a bit too weak to raise for value or as a pure bluff, but it has some connectedness. Checking allows me to see a free flop in position post-flop and try to hit a straight draw or two pair.

The board of 3-4-3 is paired and dry, which makes it difficult to improve my hand. My Queen-high has very little showdown value, and betting would be an unnecessary bluff against the big blind's range, which likely includes many cards that connect with this board. Checking is the most prudent move to control the pot size and see the turn cheaply.

The flop comes 3-3-4, which likely missed most of Flashlight's limping range. After their check, they are demonstrating weakness. I currently have 8-high with virtually no showdown value, so checking behind essentially gives up the pot unless I spike a fortunate turn card. A half-pot stab here is a low-risk, high-reward bluff that can easily fold out better high-card hands (like Jack or Queen high) that missed the board.

The flop of 3-4-3 is a very dry, paired board. My Queen-high hand has absolutely no showdown value and lacks any meaningful equity, such as a straight or flush draw. The opponent (Big Blind) has led with a bet after checking pre-flop, suggesting they at least have a piece of this board or are testing me. Calling here with no pair and no draw is a losing play in the long run. Folding allows me to preserve my stack of 5650 chips for a better, higher-equity situation.

I am in the button position with 9c 5c. This is a very weak hand, and in a three-handed game, I should be disciplined with my starting hands to preserve my stack of 5,650 chips. With both Pro and Flash being aggressive players, attempting to steal the blinds with such a marginal holding is not worth the risk. Folding is the most prudent decision here.

Q4s is a very weak hand to play out of position, even blind versus blind. While mixing in limps or raises is sometimes theoretically sound, folding here is a standard, low-variance play that avoids bleeding chips against the big stack.
Q2 offsuit is a very weak starting hand, especially considering we are the first to act on the Button. The long-term profitable play is simply to fold and wait for better opportunities.

I am in the Small Blind with K5s after the Button folded. K5s is a very strong hand for a blind-vs-blind battle. I will raise to 300 to exert pressure on the Big Blind and potentially take the pot pre-flop or lead the hand on the flop.

I am in the big blind with 3-10 offsuit, which is a weak hand with very poor playability. Even though I have already posted 100 chips and the call is only 200 more into a 400 pot, my hand does not perform well post-flop, and Flash's raise from the small blind suggests a decent range. Folding is the most disciplined and profitable play to conserve my stack.

I am on the button with an Ace (A3o) in a three-handed game. As the chip leader, I should look to apply pressure on the shorter stacks, especially against the Small Blind who has about 55 big blinds. Raising to 300 is a standard open from the button to pick up the blinds or play a pot in position.

Holding 4h 5d in the small blind against a button open is a clear fold. The hand has poor equity, and playing out of position against an aggressive opponent like Flash is ill-advised. It is better to conserve my stack for a more favorable situation.

Holding J3s in the Big Blind facing a 3x open from the Button is too weak of a hand to defend, even considering we are suited. Playing out of position with such a disconnected hand will lead to difficult post-flop spots, so folding is the optimal and standard decision here.

Pocket Jacks are a premium holding and I am in the best position on the button. Raising to 300 is a standard move to build the pot, gain initiative, and put pressure on the blinds. This is consistent with my strategy of aggressive play in late position.
T2s is a very weak starting hand. Facing a 3x raise from the button, playing this hand out of position from the small blind is unprofitable. Folding is the standard and optimal play.

I am holding 6-3 offsuit in the big blind, which is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. Flashlight has raised from the button, which is a standard steal position, but 6-3 lacks the playability or high-card strength to justify a defend even with the 3:1 pot odds. I am the chip leader and see no reason to invest chips in a marginal spot out of position with a trash hand.

KTh is a very strong suited broadway hand. Being on the button in a 3-handed game makes this a clear open raise. I'll use my standard open-raising size of 2.5x the big blind (250 chips) to try to steal the blinds or build a pot with positional advantage.

I am in the Small Blind with 8-5 offsuit, which is a very weak hand. The player on the Button (Pro) has raised to 250. Calling with such a marginal hand out of position is generally a losing play, especially with the Big Blind still to act. Given my large stack, there is no need to defend with such a weak range here.

JsTs is a strong suited connector with good playability. Facing a 2.5x open from the button, I am getting excellent pot odds (150 to win a total pot of 550) to call and see a flop. I prefer calling over 3-betting here to maintain a reasonable pot size while out of position.

I have flopped a pair of Tens and a flush draw on a King-high board. This is a very strong hand with significant equity. Being out of position as the Big Blind, checking is the standard play to gauge the button's action. If the opponent bets, I have a clear continuation plan with this level of equity.

I have flopped top two pair (Kings and Tens) on a somewhat wet board that features a flush draw (spades) and some straight draws. Flashlight checked to me, so I want to bet for value and to protect my hand against these draws. Betting around 300 (roughly 55% of the pot) will charge worse Kings, Tens, and draws to see the turn.

I have flopped a pair of Tens along with a flush draw, giving me significant equity on this board. With four spades present (my Js, Ts, plus the 6s, Ks on the board), I have a strong semi-bluffing hand that can also win at showdown with the pair of Tens. Calling the 300-chip bet allows me to see the turn at a good price while keeping the pot manageable, rather than bloating it with a raise when I am technically second-best to a pair of Kings.

The board has become more coordinated with the 7s on the turn, giving me both a pair of Tens and a flush draw. As the pre-flop aggressor (Pro) led on the flop, the standard and most strategic play while out of position is to check, allowing me to gauge their action and manage the pot size. Betting here would likely fold out hands I currently beat or force a raise from stronger hands, while checking keeps my range protected and allows me to realize my significant equity.

The 7s brings a third spade to the board, completing flush draws that might have called my flop bet. Although I have top two pair, I lack a spade for a redraw. Betting here could isolate myself against flushes and better hands, while forcing folds from worse hands I want to keep in. Checking behind controls the pot size and ensures I see a free river, where I can still hit a full house or evaluate a safe runout.

I have a Jack-high flush (K-J-T-7-6) on a board of Tc-6s-Ks-7s-2h. I held a flush since the turn, and the turn went check-check, indicating that my opponent (Pro) likely does not have a high spade or a very strong hand they wanted to bet. On this river, checking would likely lead to a check-behind from the opponent, forfeiting value. Given the strength of my hand, I must value-bet to extract chips from any King-high or pair-based holdings that my opponent might hold.

On the river, the board is Ks Tc 6s 7s 2h. I have top two pair (Kings and Tens). Flashlight leads for about half the pot (600) after the turn checked through. Because I checked the turn, my hand looks like a marginal one-pair hand (like QQ, JJ, or a weak King) or a given-up draw. This can easily induce bluffs from missed draws (like QJ) or thin value bets from worse Kx hands (like KQ or KJ). While the flush and the 8-9 straight completed on the turn, Flashlight's turn check makes it slightly less likely they hold a monster, though they could be trapping. Getting very good pot odds (600 to win 1750), my top two pair is well under-represented and strong enough to call. Raising is an overplay, as Flashlight will generally only call with hands that beat me (flushes, straights, sets) and fold their bluffs and weaker value bets.

I am on the button with 4c 5h, which is a very weak starting hand. While I have a significant chip lead and being on the button often warrants wide opening ranges, 45 offsuit is at the bottom of the range and generally not worth the risk against two opponents with 70BB stacks who have shown a willingness to defend and 3-bet. I will wait for a better spot to utilize my chip lead.

Ten-four offsuit is a very weak hand with poor playability, and playing it out of position against the Big Blind is generally not a profitable long-term strategy. Folding is the disciplined choice to conserve my stack.

I am on the button with a weak hand (10-3 offsuit). Playing this hand, even in a 3-handed game, is likely to result in a negative expected value situation post-flop. I should fold and wait for a stronger starting hand to enter the pot.

Holding 5h 2s, one of the weakest hands in Texas Hold'em, in the Small Blind. With no position and very poor equity against any calling range, attempting to steal the Big Blind is unprofitable. Folding is the optimal decision.

A6 suited is a solid hand to open-raise from the Button in a 3-handed game. It has good top pair potential and nut flush potential. I will make a standard 2.5x raise to steal the blinds or play a heads-up pot in position.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Trey suited, which is a strong hand in three-handed play. The Button (Pro) has opened for a standard raise. With my large stack, I want to apply pressure and potentially win the pot immediately, or build a pot where I have the initiative and a hand with good post-flop playability (nut flush potential and an Ace blocker). I will three-bet to 850.

I am in the Big Blind with a weak offsuit hand (10-4). There is an initial raise from the button and a 3-bet from the small blind. The pot odds do not justify calling with such a marginal hand, and I would be playing out of position against two players who have shown aggression. Folding is the mathematically correct decision.

I opened with A6s on the button, which is a standard steal, but the Small Blind 3-bet to 850. A6s is generally too weak to call a 3-bet as it suffers from reverse implied odds against a typical 3-betting range (we can easily be outkicked if an Ace hits). We have a healthy 70bb stack and no reason to force the action. Folding is the most prudent decision.

I am the chip leader with 16,300 chips. The two remaining opponents, Flashlight and Pro, have significantly smaller stacks (around 66-69 BBs). 8d 4d is a marginal suited hand. While it's tempting to open from the button to steal the blinds, both opponents have shown a tendency to be active and defend or 3-bet (especially Flashlight, who recently played a massive pot with J6o). Given the low rank of my cards and the aggression of the blinds, I will choose to fold this hand and wait for a better spot to utilize my chip lead.

I am in the small blind with a strong hand (Ace-Jack offsuit) in a heads-up situation against the big blind after the button folded. Raising is the optimal move here to take control of the pot, as AJo is a high-equity hand that should be played aggressively pre-flop to maximize value or force a fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Jc 2h, facing a 3x raise from the Small Blind. Jack-deuce offsuit is a very weak hand with practically no playability post-flop, even with position. The standard and most profitable play here is to simply fold and wait for a better spot.
A-7 offsuit is a strong hand in a 3-handed game on the button. Raising to 300 is a standard move to open the pot, attempt to steal the blinds, and take the initiative in the hand.

I have 64o in the small blind facing a 3x raise from the button. This hand is completely unplayable, unsuited, and disconnected. Furthermore, I would be out of position for the rest of the hand. An easy fold.

I am currently the chip leader with a comfortable stack. I am in the big blind with 52 offsuit, which is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. Flashlight has raised to 300 from the button. While I am getting decent pot odds to call, playing 52o out of position against a button raise is generally a losing play in the long run. There is no need for me to defend such a weak hand and risk chips in a marginal situation when I have the lead.

J7s is a bit too weak and marginal to open even on the button in this 3-handed dynamic, especially when we want to avoid getting 3-bet by the aggressive big stacks. I'll fold and wait for a better spot.

In a 3-handed game, when the button folds, the Small Blind should open a wide range of hands to put pressure on the Big Blind. Queen-Seven suited (Qd 7d) is a strong starting hand in a blind-vs-blind scenario. I have a significant chip lead over Flashlight (16,150 vs 6,850), which allows me to play aggressively. Raising to 300 is a standard open that aims to either win the blinds immediately or take control of the hand post-flop with a hand that has decent equity and playability.

The opponent, Flash, raised from the small blind. Holding Ace-four offsuit in the big blind is a standard defensive hand. Since I am heads-up and out of position, calling the raise to 300 is the most prudent play to see a flop, rather than re-raising with a marginal Ace-high hand.

On a board of 6c-5d-4s, I have an open-ended straight draw with my 7d (needing a 3 or an 8 to complete the straight) and one overcard (the Queen). While this low, connected board texture is often favorable for the Big Blind's calling range, I want to maintain the initiative as the pre-flop aggressor. A continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) allows me to potentially win the pot immediately or build it for when I hit my draw on later streets. My stack advantage also allows me to apply this pressure comfortably.

The flop is 6-5-4, which is extremely connected and favors the small blind's wider range. I have bottom pair (pair of 4s) with an Ace kicker. Given the pot odds (getting 3:1 on a call) and the fact that I have some backdoor equity, calling is the most standard play. Raising here would likely only get called by better hands (like 5x, 6x, or pocket pairs) or make me commit chips to a dangerous board where I am already behind a significant portion of the opponent's range.

On the turn 9 of diamonds, my hand has improved significantly. I now have both an open-ended straight draw (needing an 8 or 3) and a flush draw (holding Qd 7d with two diamonds on the board). This gives me approximately 15 outs to a very strong hand. Given the coordinated nature of the board, I want to continue representing a strong range (like overpairs or sets) while maximizing my fold equity. A bet of 800 into a 1200 pot (2/3 pot) applies significant pressure on Flashlight's marginal made hands, such as middle pairs or weak top pairs, while I still have plenty of equity to win the hand even if called.

Any 5, 6, 9, 7-8, or better pocket pairs have me beaten. With Flash betting 800 into a 1200 pot, the price is too high to call for a hand with minimal drawing equity. Folding is the disciplined choice.

I am on the button with 9d 5c. While I have a significant chip lead, this hand is too weak to justify an open-raise against two active opponents with over 60 big blinds each. Folding is the most disciplined play to maintain my stack for better opportunities.

I am in the small blind with a very weak hand, 2-3 offsuit. With the button having folded, I am heads-up against the big blind, but there is no strategic value in playing such a poor hand out of position when I can simply fold and preserve my stack.

I am on the button with Ace-Ten suited, which is a strong hand in a three-handed game. I am in the Small Blind with 94 offsuit, which is a very weak starting hand. Flashlight raised to 300 from the Button. This is a clear fold to preserve my stack.

In the Big Blind, I am holding 9-8 offsuit. The button (Flashlight) has opened to 300. On a paired flop of 6-6-7, I have an open-ended straight draw with my 9-8 (needing a 5 or a 10). The board is paired (6-6-7), which significantly reduces the likelihood that either player has connected strongly unless they hold a six or a seven. My Ace-Ten is currently just Ace-high. While I have the betting lead, betting into a paired board with overcards is risky, as it allows my opponent to check-raise me and force me to fold my equity. Checking back allows me to take a free card, keep the pot size manageable, and realize my equity while controlling the betting variance. I will check to see the turn card.

On a board of 6-6-7-4, I have an open-ended straight draw (8 outs). Flashlight checked back on the flop, which suggests they likely don't have a 6 or a strong 7. While I could bet to represent a 6 or a 7 myself, checking is the most prudent play here as the chip leader. It allows me to see a free river if Flashlight checks again, or to evaluate their bet size if they decide to lead. Given I have significant equity with my draw, I am comfortable checking and calling a reasonable bet to see the final card.

The board is 6-6-7-4. I have Ace-Ten high. My opponent has checked to me, which suggests they either have a weak hand or are checking to trap with a six. Betting into this board does not generate much value, as I am unlikely to get called by hands worse than mine that aren't pure bluffs. Checking behind allows me to realize my showdown equity cheaply and avoid bloating a pot on a paired board where my Ace-high is vulnerable to any pair.

I have rivered top pair (nines) on a board that has been checked through on the flop and turn. Given the lack of aggression from Flashlight, it's unlikely they hold a 6 or a strong 7. Betting for value is the right move here, as I can get called by Ace-high, a 7, or lower pocket pairs. A bet of around 60% pot (400) is a good sizing to extract value without scaring off marginal hands.

The opponent, Flash, has checked through the flop and turn, and is now leading for 400 into a 650 pot on the river. This line of check-check-check-bet often signals a polarized range, particularly a missed straight draw like 8-5 or 5-3, or simply complete air. My Ace-Ten high is a strong bluff-catcher in this spot, as it beats all the missed draws in the opponent's range and is only losing to made pairs or better. Given the pot odds and the board texture (6-6-7-4-9), which is difficult to have connected with firmly without having a six or a seven (which the opponent likely would have played differently), calling is the most profitable play to capitalize on a likely bluff.

Ace-Five offsuit is a marginal hand. While it could potentially be opened on the Button in 3-handed play as a steal, the chip leader is in the Small Blind and the Big Blind has a 55 bb stack that could play back aggressively. To avoid marginal spots and reverse implied odds, I prefer to simply fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am in the Small Blind with 6-3 offsuit, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. With the Button (Pro) folding, the action is on me. While I could attempt a blind steal by raising or complete the bet to see a flop, 6-3o has very poor playability and equity out of position. Given my significant chip lead, there is no need to play trash hands. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play in this situation.

I am on the button with T6o. While it's a marginal hand, in a three-handed game it's often worth an opening raise to steal the blinds. With the button (Flash) folding, I am heads-up against the big blind in the small blind position. Ace-Eight offsuit is a strong hand for this scenario. Facing a Small Blind raise, we have a premium suited connector (6c 7c) in the Big Blind. We will be in position post-flop against the raiser. The pot odds and implied odds make this a highly profitable call, allowing us to evaluate the flop and use our positional advantage.

Facing a continuation bet on a dry Q-high flop, our 7-high has virtually no equity. Without any direct draws and only weak backdoor potential, floating or bluff-raising is unnecessary here. Folding is the optimal decision.

Q-7 offsuit is a marginal hand, but as the button in a 3-handed game, this is a prime opportunity to steal the blinds and potentially build my stack. I am currently the shortest stack, so I need to be proactive. A standard raise to 300 is a calculated risk to win the 150 already in the pot, and I have the positional advantage if called.

I have 87 offsuit in the Small Blind facing a 3x raise from the Button. 87o is too weak and lacks the playability to call or 3-bet from out of position, especially with the Big Blind still left to act behind me. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here to preserve my stack.

I am in the Big Blind with 8h 5d, a very weak hand. Flashlight opened for a 3x raise from the button. 52o is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Being first to act on the Button with this hand, the optimal play is simply to fold and wait for a better opportunity.

In a blind vs. blind situation with K6o after the Button has folded, my hand is relatively strong. While I will be out of position post-flop, K6o is well ahead of the average random hand. I am in the Big Blind with Qs4s, facing a raise to 300 from the Small Blind. The pot is currently 400, and it costs 200 to call. The flop of A-T-T is extremely dry and highly favorable to the pre-flop aggressor's range. My hand, Q-4 suited, has absolutely no showdown value, no pair, and no meaningful draw. I am holding 3c 5d on the button. While I have a significant chip lead, 3-5 offsuit is one of the weakest hands in the game and generally not worth playing even for a steal attempt. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my stack advantage.

I am in the Small Blind with pocket nines, a strong heads-up hand. Since the Button folded, I am against the Big Blind. With 73o in the Big Blind facing a 3x raise from the Small Blind, my hand is far too weak to defend. Unsuited, unconnected, and bottom-tier cards like this have virtually no post-flop playability and very poor equity against any legitimate opening range. Folding is the only viable option.

I am in the button position with Queen-Eight offsuit, which is a marginal but playable hand in a three-handed game. I am in the Big Blind with KQo on a low, somewhat connected board of 3c-6c-8d. As the caller in a heads-up pot out of position, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. K4o is a very weak starting hand. Even though I have position on the Button, this hand has poor playability and low equity against any calling or raising ranges from the blinds. Folding is the standard and correct play.

K9o is a strong enough starting hand to play aggressively in a blind-vs-blind situation. By raising to 300 from the small blind, I apply pressure on the big blind to fold marginal hands and take down the pot uncontested, while still having a decent holding if called.

My King-high has very little equity against his continuing range. Firing a second barrel here is unlikely to fold out a Queen or any made pairs, and it unnecessarily bloats the pot out of position with a weak holding. The turn 2c pairs the board, and Pro has checked after betting the flop. My Ace-high still holds significant showdown value against Pro's possible range of missed draws or lower high cards. I am in position and happy to reach a cheap showdown or evaluate the river based on Pro's next action.

On the river, the Ten of Clubs completes the board. Flash's flop call and turn check back suggest a range composed of weak pairs (like 4x, 55-99), Ace-high floats, or complete air that missed. Attempting a bluff here to fold out Ace-high or a stubborn small pair is unnecessarily risky, especially since my line of bet-check doesn't strongly represent a big hand that improved on this specific river. Checking allows me to reach showdown for free, occasionally winning against complete air, while preserving my stack by folding if faced with a bet.

On the river, the board is Q-4-2-2-T. Pro has checked for the second street in a row after betting the flop. Since Pro has shown weakness by checking the turn and river, I see no reason to turn my hand into a bluff or risk a check-raise by betting. Checking back allows me to reach a showdown and win the pot if my Ace-high is best, which is quite possible given the action.
Queen-Ten offsuit is a standard opening hand from the button in a 3-handed game. I will raise to 2.5x the big blind to try and steal the blinds or take control of the hand post-flop.

I am in the Small Blind with 7s4h.
